macro_segments.title: Multiple Sin Offerings (Chatasos) and Guilt Offerings (Ashamim): Categories and Requirements

micro_segments.title: Overview: Types of Chatas and Asham Offerings
The Three Cases of Asham (Definite Guilt Offering): Me'ila, Theft and False Oath, Shifcha Charufa
Asham Talui (Doubtful Guilt Offering) and Asham Safek (Doubtful Sin Offering)
Rabbi Akiva vs. Rabbi Tarfon: Can You Bring an Asham Safek for a Sin Requiring Asham Vadai?
The Gemara's Inference: Asham Vadai Does Not Require Prior Knowledge (Yedia Batchila)

macro_segments.title: Multiple Acts of Shifcha Charufa and the Significance of Awareness in Dividing Obligatory Offerings

micro_segments.title: Five Acts with a Half-Free Slave Woman: One or Multiple Sin Offerings?
Ulah's Position: Knowledge Not Required for Asham Vadai; Awareness Does Not Divide Acts
Rav Hamnuna's Challenge: The Case of Designating a Korban Between Acts
Response: Designating a Korban Creates Division, But Awareness Alone Does Not

macro_segments.title: Rav Dimi's Opposing View: Knowledge Required for Asham Vadai; Awareness Divides Acts

micro_segments.title: Rav Dimi's Claim: If Knowledge Is Required, Multiple Acts of Shifcha Charufa Require Multiple Offerings
Abaye's Analogy: The Debate of Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish on Separate Awarenesses
Unpacking the Comparison: Awareness Between Acts vs. Awareness After Acts
Clarification: According to Reish Lakish, Only Hafrasha (Designation) Divides, Not Awareness

macro_segments.title: Ravin's Summary: Consensus and Dispute Regarding Knowledge and Division of Acts in Shifcha Charufa

micro_segments.title: Hakol Modim (All Agree) When Knowledge Not Required: One Offering Even for Multiple Acts
Hakol Modim (All Agree) When Hafrasha Occurs: Multiple Offerings When Korban Designated Between Acts
Machloket (Dispute) When Knowledge Is Required: Rabbi Yochanan vs. Reish Lakish on Awareness Dividing Acts

macro_segments.title: Intentionality and Incidental Acts (Mitasek) on Shabbat: The Debate of Rava and Abaye

micro_segments.title: Defining Mitasek: Completely Incidental Acts Versus Unintentional Acts (Shogeg)
Case One: Intending to Lift Detached Object but Cutting Attached Object (Separate Acts)
Case Two: Intending to Cut but Mistaking Detached for Attached (Rava vs. Abaye Debate)
Rava's Position: Mistake of Fact About Object Status Exempts from Obligation
Abaye's Position: Knowledge of the Act Itself Creates Obligation Despite Factual Mistake

macro_segments.title: Comparison of Stringencies: Shabbat Versus Other Mitzvot (Chomer and Tafel)

micro_segments.title: Chomer Shabbat (Stringency of Shabbat): Multiple Melachot in One Forgetfulness Require Multiple Offerings
Clarification: Two Different Categories (Kotzer and Tochein) Versus Two Acts of Same Category
Analogy to Other Mitzvot: Avodah Zarah as Example Where Multiple Acts Yield One Offering

macro_segments.title: Chomer She'ar Mitzvot (Stringency of Other Mitzvot): Unintentional Transgression Without Intention

micro_segments.title: Shogeg B'lo Mitkaven in Other Mitzvot: Case of Mistaking Permissible Fat for Forbidden Fat
Exploring What Shogeg B'lo Mitkaven Means in Avodah Zarah Context
Case: Bowing in Synagogue Thinking It Is Holy (No Act of Avodah Zarah Intended)
Case: Bowing to Statue Without Accepting It as God (Not True Avodah Zarah)
Case: Worshipping from Ahava and Yirah (Love of Gain or Fear) Without Belief in Divinity
Rava's Reading: Omer Mutar—Thinking Avodah Zarah Is Permissible (Standard Shogeg)
Analogy to Shabbat: Thinking Detached Versus Attached; Rava's Exemption vs. Abaye's Obligation

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Counter-Argument: When Different Acts Versus Same Act With Factual Mistake

micro_segments.title: Abaye's Case: Thinking Swallowing Spit, Actually Swallowing Forbidden Fat (Completely Different Acts)
Principle: Deriving Benefit in Sex and Food Creates Ownership of Act Despite Unknowing Intent
Mitasek Does Not Apply to Sex and Food (Chalavin V'Arayot) Due to Deriving Benefit
Summary: Rava Exempts Mistake of Fact; Abaye Obligates When Same Act Intended Regardless of Fact

macro_segments.title: Discussion of Ahava and Yirah in Avodah Zarah and the Principle of Yehareg V'al Ya'avor (Martyrdom)

micro_segments.title: Technical Question: If Ahava and Yirah Exempt, How Does Yehareg V'al Ya'avor Apply?
Tosafot's Approach: Limiting Rava's Case to When Non-Belief Is Obvious
Alternative Explanation: Yehareg V'al Ya'avor Derives From Love of God, Not Technical Avodah Zarah
